title: Trade Secret Protection and the Integration of Information Within Firms
abstract: We examine the effect of trade secret protection laws on internal information integration (i.e., the extent to which economic agents are provided with access to decision-relevant information from other economic agents within a firm). We argue that stronger trade secret protection laws increase firms’ internal information integration because they reduce the proprietary costs of information leakage. To test our prediction, we measure a firm’s internal information integration by the share of its sites integrated into its enterprise management system. Exploiting the staggered adoption of trade secret protection laws via the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), we find that these laws increase firms’ internal information integration. This effect is stronger (weaker) for firms with higher proprietary costs (coordination benefits). Further, we provide evidence that the UTSA-induced increase in internal information integration translates into improvements of firms’ internal information quality and decision-making quality. Taken together, our results enhance the understanding of the economic trade-offs shaping firms’ internal information environment.

article.1. Introduction: Under the knowledge-based view of the firm, a key advantage of firms over markets is their ability to create, exploit, and protect proprietary information (e.g.,Liebeskind 1996,1997). Within the boundaries of a firm, value can be generated by exchanging and using proprietary information without making it public (Robertson et al. 2015). However, firm boundaries are unlikely to perfectly protect a firm’s proprietary information; corporate insiders (such as employees) constitute the most common source of information leakage, posing a major threat to a firm’s competitive advantage (InfoWatch 2006,2014,2018;Cheng et al. 2013;Wong et al. 2019;Cabezon and Hoberg 2022). Accordingly, firms need to trade off the benefits of internally exchanging and exploiting their proprietary information against the costs associated with its increased leakage risk to outsiders. In this paper, we study whether leakage costs affect firms’ internal information integration (i.e., the extent to which firms grant their economic agents access to decision-relevant proprietary information from other economic agents within the firm). To that end, we focus on variation in leakage costs stemming from the protection of trade secrets. Trade secrets are among firms’ most valuable assets (Shapiro and Hassett 2005), and their leakage constitutes one of the biggest threats to firms’ competitiveness (Wong et al. 2019).1To prevent such leakage from occurring, firms often rely on secrecy and restrict their economic agents’ access to proprietary internal information (i.e., reduce internal information integration) (Cohen et al. 2000,Arundel 2001,Png 2017b). Another key mechanism of protecting trade secrets is trade secret protection laws (Ronde 2001). These laws enable firms to legally contest the misappropriation of trade secrets, thus reducing both the damage and the likelihood of such misappropriation by unauthorized third parties, like competitors (Franzoni and Kaushik 2016,Glaeser 2018,Li et al. 2018). We focus on a specific trade secret protection law in the United States: the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). Enacted at the state level, the UTSA extends the remedies that firms can obtain in case of trade secret misappropriation and facilitates litigation, thus providing for stronger trade secret protection (Png 2017b,Glaeser 2018). We therefore argue that the UTSA reduces firms’ expected cost of information leakage, causing them to increase their internal information integration (Ronde 2001,Baldwin and Henkel 2015,Elliott et al. 2019). In our empirical tests, we measure firms’ internal information integration by the share of sites2that is integrated into a firm’s enterprise management system (EMS). We focus on EMSs because they are a key technology allowing firms to grant their agents access to decision-relevant information (Bloom et al. 2014). Importantly, the type of information typically integrated into EMSs—such as customer information (e.g., contact details), pricing information (e.g., product costs), or personnel information (e.g., salaries)—is covered by the increased protection of the UTSA. We focus on the share of integrated sites because the number of site managers who can contribute to and access firm-wide information increases as more sites are integrated into the firm’s EMS. At the same time, as more sites are integrated into the EMS, a greater number of economic agents can potentially leak this information. Thus, we believe that the share of integrated sites is an important choice of the firm, on which the reduction in leakage costs because of trade secret protection laws has a first-order impact. We label this variableEMS integration. To identify the effect of the UTSA onEMS integration, we employ a difference-in-differences (DID) design around states’ enactments of the UTSA over the period from 1999 to 2009. During this period, the enactment of the UTSA affected sample firms in two states (i.e., Pennsylvania and Tennessee). We find that, following the enactment of the UTSA, treatment firms significantly increase theirEMS integrationrelative to control firms from states with no UTSA enactment. In cross-sectional tests, we corroborate information leakage costs as the channel through which this effect arises. Specifically, we find that the UTSA increases firms’EMS integrationmore (less) when they have higher marginal costs (benefits) of internal information integration. Collectively, these findings are consistent with our prediction that trade secret protection laws increase firms’ internal information integration via reducing their marginal costs of information leakage. In further analyses, we corroborate our approach of usingEMS integrationas a measure of firms’ internal information integration and document howEMS integrationcontributes to the quality of the internal information environment. Specifically, we use path analysis to document the direct and indirect effects stemming from the adoption of the UTSA on several proxies for firms’ internal information quality (IIQ) and decision-making quality (DMQ), respectively. We do not find a significant direct path from the UTSA to these proxies, consistent with idea that the UTSA affects firms’ internal information environment via multiple offsetting channels. However, when we focus on the indirect path viaEMS integration, we find a significant positive relation between the UTSA and firms’ IIQ and DMQ, respectively. This result supports the notion that internal information integration, measured byEMS integration, is an important input to both IIQ and DMQ, via which trade secret protection laws can matter for firms’ internal information environment more broadly. In our robustness tests, we address two threats to our inferences. For one, a concern might be that our main result is confounded by omitted correlated variables. In addition to the features of our main research design that speak to this concern (such as our granular fixed effects structure, firm- and state-level time-variant controls, and event-time analyses), we provide several further pieces of evidence. First, we show that the positive relation between the UTSA andEMS integrationholds for each of our two treatment states individually, mitigating concerns about other simultaneous events in a particular state. Second, we find that the positive effect of the UTSA is robust to different choices of control states, including a restricted set of states that have not enacted the UTSA by the end of our sample period. This finding alleviates concerns that our main result stems from fundamental differences between treatment and control firms, which may correlate with states’ enactment of the UTSA before the beginning of our sample period. Third, we find thatEMS integrationis negatively associated with data breach notification laws, which increase leakage concerns as they require firms to publicly disclose data breaches. This implies that for an omitted correlated variable to confound our results, it would need to positively correlate with the UTSA and negatively correlate with data breach notification laws. Fourth, we show that our results are not driven by time-varying factors at the state and site level. Finally, our tests using the impact threshold for a correlated variable (ITCV) and robustness of inferences to replacement (RIR) suggest that it is unlikely that there is an omitted correlated variable that would render our inferences invalid. For another, a concern might be that the UTSA affects firms’ internal information integration via channels other than leakage costs (e.g., alleviated financial constraints). To address this concern, we provide two sets of tests. First, we construct an alternative dependent variable that captures firms’ use of software applications that are integrated in an EMS relative to their overall use of software applications (either integrated or stand-alone). We label this variableApplication integration ratio. Similar to our main result, we find that the UTSA increases firms’Application integration ratios. This finding is inconsistent with any alternative channel that would affect firms’ overall investment in software applications (either integrated or stand-alone) because such a channel would impact both the numerator and the denominator of ourApplication integration ratio. Second, we provide evidence that financial constraints or external reporting incentives are not the main channel via which the UTSA affects firms’EMS integration(Acharya et al. 2016). We contribute to the literature in the following ways. First, we add to prior research on the frictions that impair information exchange between economic agents. Studies in this stream of literature emphasize that proprietary costs matter for firms’ external reporting to investors (e.g.,Berger and Hann 2007,Bens et al. 2011,Li et al. 2018) and for information exchange between firms (e.g.,Asker and Ljungqvist 2010,Anderson et al. 2014,Christ and Nicolaou 2016,Bills et al. 2020). Whether these results generalize to the integration of informationwithin firmsis, however, unclear because firm boundaries could be an effective mechanism to protect proprietary information (Liebeskind 1997). Prior studies have provided analytical (Ronde 2001) and qualitative (Elliott et al. 2019) analyses on the effectiveness of firm boundaries for protecting firms’ proprietary information. We provide large-scale empirical evidence on the role of leakage costs and their reduction via trade secret protection laws for firms’ decisions to integrate internal information. Our study thereby complementsGlaeser et al. (2023), who study where firms locate their inventors to facilitate knowledge flows in the presence of a different type of leakage concern (namely, labor market competitors). Second, we contribute to opening the black box of what constitutes firms’ internal information environment. To that end, we construct a proxy of firms’ internal information integration based on their share of sites integrated into an EMS. This measure reflects an important choice variable that firms can readily adjust in response to shifts in expected costs and benefits. Thus, it provides us with a suitable setting to test theoretical predictions concerning the trade-offs that influence firms’ decisions to integrate internal information (Ronde 2001). By focusing on a relevant and observableinputto the quality of firms’ internal information environment, we further complement prior research usingoutcomesrelated to IIQ or DMQ (e.g.,Gallemore and Labro 2015,Lyle 2019). Third, we contribute to studies on the determinants of management accounting systems (e.g.,Chenhall and Morris 1986,Ittner et al. 2002,Abernethy and Bouwens 2005,Davila and Foster 2005,Amiraslani et al. 2024). Using mainly survey evidence, this literature focuses on variation in internal information demand and how it determines the design of firms’ management accounting systems. Other studies have similarly focused on the implementation of EMSs and how they enrich the internal information environment (e.g.,Cassar and Gibson 2008,Heitzman and Huang 2019,Labro and Stice-Lawrence 2020,Labro et al. 2022). We complement these studies by providing large-scale, archival evidence on an important strategic cost factor, namely the costs of information leakage. Finally, our study contributes novel evidence on the consequences of trade secret protection laws. Prior research finds that trade secret protection laws are associated with employee mobility and knowledge spillovers (Png 2017a), patenting (Png 2017b,Contigiani et al. 2018,Glaeser 2018), investment in knowledge assets (Qiu and Wang 2018), disclosure (Glaeser 2018,Li et al. 2018), and firm value (Klasa et al. 2018,Qiu and Wang 2018). More broadly, our findings contribute to the growing literature on accounting and innovation (Glaeser et al. 2023). Specifically, our results suggest an increase in internal information integration as a response to stronger protection of innovation and trade secrets.
article.2. Theoretical Background, Prior Literat: 2.1. Prior Literature on Information Exchange Between Economic Agents Our study relates to the literature on frictions that impair information exchange between economic agents. Prior studies document that proprietary costs prevent information exchangeacrossfirm boundaries. For instance,Christ and Nicolaou (2016)examine the use of integrated information systems in interfirm alliances. They investigate a trade-off similar to our trade-off as alliance partners need to weigh the benefits of integrated information systems (improved coordination, control, and communication within the alliance) against the costs of information exchange risk, including the proprietary costs arising from partners using the shared information opportunistically. Consistent with such trade-off, the evidence inChrist and Nicolaou (2016)suggests that firms use formal controls (e.g., policies, rules, and reporting structures) to mitigate information exchange risk.Anderson et al. (2014)discuss firms’ practice of fragmenting proprietary information that is shared in alliances to mitigate the risk of information leakage. Relatedly,Clemons and Hitt (2004)discuss the concept of “poaching” (i.e., the opportunistic use of information by alliance partners) and highlight the resulting economic inefficiencies. Other studies emphasize that proprietary cost concerns prevent firms from exchanging information with specific economic agents, such as auditors (Bills et al. 2020) or banks (von Rheinbaben and Ruckes 2004,Asker and Ljungqvist 2010), that might leak such information to firms’ competitors. As a consequence, firms incur higher costs in terms of audit or banking fees. Whereas information exchange risk and resulting economic inefficiencies are prominently discussed and analyzed in the context ofinterfirmrelations, it is not clear whether similar concerns apply tointrafirmsettings. Similar to the use of contracts or formal controls in interfirm relations, firm boundaries can serve as an effective mechanism to mitigate proprietary costs of information leakage (Liebeskind 1997,Elliott et al. 2019). Yet, firm boundaries might not protect firms’ proprietary information perfectly. Indeed, practitioner surveys highlight that leakage is predominantly attributable to corporate insiders (InfoWatch 2006,2014,2018)3and causes major business costs (Wong et al. 2019). In line with practitioners’ perceptions,Cabezon and Hoberg (2022)provide large-scale evidence that leakage of proprietary information to competitors through an important corporate insider (i.e., directors) lowers the profits and valuation outcomes of the affected firms. Finally,Glaeser et al. (2023)provide evidence suggesting that firms trade off the benefits of increased information flows against leakage costs (e.g., from potential labor market competitors) in their decisions on the location of inventors. If firm boundaries fail to sufficiently protect firms’ proprietary information, the resulting leakage concerns may affect firms’ decision to integrate information internally. Consistent with this idea,Liebeskind (1997),Hannah (2005),Baldwin and Henkel (2015), andRobertson et al. (2015)provide qualitative arguments that firms restrict information provided to their managers in the presence of leakage concerns. Closely related to our research question,Ronde (2001)provides an analytical model of how expected leakage costs affect a firm’s choice of organizational form (i.e., joint production or task separation). Under joint production, more employees have access to proprietary information and trade secrets. As a result, joint production is preferable to task separation in terms of operating efficiency, but also, it has higher leakage costs. These leakage costs arise because under joint production, it is more likely that employees with access to trade secrets are poached by competitors. Thus, the firm needs to trade off the benefits of better economic decisions against the costs of revealing proprietary information to competitors. Consistent with such trade-off, the author demonstrates that a firm’s choice of joint production over task separation depends on the efficiency loss from task separation and the competitive environment. Similarly,Elliott et al. (2019)provide case study evidence on internal information exchange across a firm’s research and development laboratories. They argue that the firm needs to trade off the benefits of improved decision making and innovation with information leakage risk. Their interview evidence suggests that relational contracts, similar to firm boundaries, importantly mitigate such leakage risk, allowing for more information exchange. In conclusion, practitioner surveys and analytical models suggest that firm boundaries might not always be sufficient to protect firms against information leakage. Yet, empirical evidence on the cost/benefit trade-offs shaping firms’ decisions on the extent to which they provide economic agents with access to internal information is scarce. We fill this gap by providing large-scale empirical evidence on the effect of leakage costs on firms’ internal information integration. 2.2. The Effect of Trade Secret Protection Laws on the Integration of Information Within Firms When deciding on the extent to which economic agents are provided with access to information from other economic agents within the firm, firms trade off the marginal costs and benefits of internal information integration. On the one hand, access to firm-wide information improves economic agents’ decision making (e.g., because they can take customer demands from other locations into account). On the other hand, the expected costs of information leakage increase as more information is made available to a greater number of economic agents (e.g., because more comprehensive, firm-wide information is more valuable to competitors and because more agents can leak such information). Thus, when deciding on their internal information integration, firms face a trade-off between the benefits of improved economic decision making and higher expected costs of information leakage. Trade secret protection laws can increase firms’ internal information integration as they reduce firms’ expected costs of information leakage. For one, these laws reduce firms’ costs resulting from trade secret misappropriation (Png 2017b,Glaeser 2018) as they enable firms to legally contest the disclosure and use of proprietary information by unauthorized parties. For another, trade secret protection laws reduce the probability of information leakage as they reduce third parties’ benefits from access to trade secrets (Franzoni and Kaushik 2016). In line with these arguments,Ronde (2001)demonstrates analytically that firms provide more employees with access to trade secrets when trade secret protection increases. There are also reasons why trade secret protection laws might not matter for firms’ internal information integration. Firms may not need to rely on regulation to protect their trade secrets, but rather, they may use other mechanisms, such as organizational design and contracting. Moreover, firm boundaries might already sufficiently prevent information leakage (Liebeskind 1996,Elliott et al. 2019), rendering the incremental reduction in leakage costs because of trade secret protection laws negligible. Inconsistent with the notion that firm boundaries serve as a sufficient mechanism to protect proprietary information, however, practitioner surveys and anecdotal evidence highlight the relevance of information leakage concerns, in particular related to trade secrets (Cohen et al. 2000,National Science Foundation, National Center for Science and Engineering Statistics 2015). Thus, in line with prior analytical work (Ronde 2001), we state the following hypothesis. Trade secret protection laws increase firms’internal information integration.
article.3. Variable Measurement and Research Des: 3.1.EMS Integration(Dependent Variable) To test our hypothesis, we need an empirical measure that can be expected to vary with the costs and benefits of internal information integration. We obtain such a measure based on the share of sites integrated into a firm’s EMS. The goal of EMSs is to automate, standardize, and integrate information across sites and functions within a firm (Ranganathan and Brown 2006,Tian and Xu 2015,Pincus et al. 2017). They provide a unified information technology (IT) infrastructure aimed at streamlining information flows, enhancing data consistency, and supporting business processes (Morris and Venkatesh 2010,Dorantes et al. 2013,Tian and Xu 2015). Importantly, as more sites are integrated into an EMS, more decision-relevant information is obtained from and distributed to more site managers within the firm. On the benefits side, integrating more sites into an EMS improves (site) managers’ decision making and related outcomes (Granlund and Malmi 2002,Ranganathan and Brown 2006,Bloom et al. 2014,Labro et al. 2022).4Further, integrating more sites into a firm’s EMS enables enhanced coordination among sites (Liebeskind 1997,Gallemore and Labro 2015,Labro et al. 2022). On the costs side, firms face considerable costs of finding, implementing, customizing, and maintaining an EMS (Dorantes et al. 2013). These costs comprise (fixed) initial implementation costs as well as the incremental costs of integrating individual sites into the EMS. In addition, integrating more sites into the EMS can give rise to proprietary costs because of increased risk of information leakage (e.g., to competitors) (Ronde 2001). Next to intentional information leakage (e.g., because of theft or disclosure to unauthorized third parties) (Huth et al. 2013,InfoWatch 2018,Molok et al. 2018), users of EMSs might leak information unintentionally through unidentified channels (Ahmad et al. 2014) or when they are uncertain about which information should not be disclosed to third parties (Molok et al. 2018). We construct our dependent variable,EMS integration, as the share of U.S. sites per firm, weighted by their number of employees, that have implemented an EMS by yeart. 3.2.Trade Secret Protection Laws(Independent Variable) We exploit variation in firms’ exposure to trade secret protection laws introduced by the staggered enactment of the UTSA across U.S. states. Over the period from 1980 to today, the UTSA has been enacted by all states with the exception of New York. The overall goal of the UTSA is to standardize, codify, and improve state laws of trade secret protection. The UTSA solidifies the protection of trade secrets in two respects. First, it considers the mere acquisition of a trade secret to be misappropriation, even if the information was not used or disclosed. Second, the UTSA provides for a broad definition of a trade secret, defining it as information that (1) derives independent economic value from not being generally known to or accessible by other people who may benefit from its use or disclosure and (2) is subject to reasonable efforts protecting its secrecy (e.g., via confidentiality agreements with employees). Trade secrets can be of a technical nature (software or techniques) and/or of strategic relevance (e.g., business plans, financial data, details about customers and suppliers, or personnel data). For example, the following information has been classified as a trade secret during UTSA rulings: customer identities (Morlife Inc. v. Perry, 56 Cal. App. 4th 1514, 1997), customer lists, preferences and contact information, pricing guidelines, historical purchasing information (Brocade Communication Systems Inc. v. A10 Networks Inc., 873 F.Supp.2d 1192; N.D. Cal. 2012), internal pricing and competition strategies (Engility Corp. v. Daniels, No. 16-cv-2473-WJM-MEH, 2016 WL 7034976, at *6, *8; D. Colo. Dec. 2, 2016), personnel and salary information(Marlite, Inc. v. Canas, No. 5:09CV1401, 2009 WL 7272686, at *11; N.D. Ohio July 22, 2009), and data compilations (of public data;AirFacts, Inc. v. de Amezaga, 909 F.3d 84, 96-97; 4th Cir. 2018). This type of information is typically stored in EMSs or local stand-alone applications, such as accounting (e.g., financial data, internal pricing, or purchasing information), human resources (HR; e.g., compensation plans), customer relationship management (CRM; e.g., customer-related information), or supply chain management (e.g., procurement data).5The definition provided by the UTSA is comprehensive as it also encompasses work in progress and negative information (e.g., information on which approaches or processes donotwork), and it does not require the information to be business related or to be in continuous use (Pooley 1997,Png 2017b). To prevent actual or threatened misappropriation, the UTSA provides injunctive relief to trade secret owners. An injunction can be long enough to ensure that no advantage from misappropriation can be gained, and it allows for punitive damages up to twice the amount of the actual damages (Png 2017b). To capture a firm’s exposure to trade secret protection laws, we follow prior literature (Castellaneta et al. 2017,Glaeser 2018) and use a dummy variable indicating whether the state of a firm’s headquarters has enacted the UTSA in the current year.6By using a dummy variable, we refrain from subjective judgments on the strength or relative importance of the UTSA relative to pre-existing common laws (Glaeser 2018). We use headquarters states because we expect a firm’s possibilities to make claims under the UTSA to substantially increase when its headquarters state enacts the UTSA. In trade secret-related disputes, courts frequently determine personal jurisdiction based on the headquarters state because it has a substantial relationship to the case (Jones 2014,Porter 2016,Li et al. 2018). Similarly, if a firm’s headquarters state has enacted the UTSA, firms can support the use of the law of the headquarters state via choice-of-law provisions (Porter 2016) and by initiating litigation in the headquarters state (Steinmeyer et al. 2019). In conclusion, we expect the enactment of the UTSA in firms’ headquarters states to have an important effect on their exposure to trade secret protection and hence, on their expected leakage cost. We confirm the importance of a firm’s headquarters state for trade secret litigation by a comprehensive, empirical examination of UTSA litigation cases involving public plaintiffs (i.e., firms similar to those in our sample) during our sample period (see Online Appendix A for details). We find that in approximately 90% (80%) of all cases that fall under state (federal) jurisdiction, courts apply the UTSA of the claimant’s headquarters state. This includes cases involving trade secrets misappropriation by sites or employees in states other than the headquarters state (see alsoBerman et al. 2017).7This empirical evidence supports the use of firms’ headquarters state to construct our explanatory variable. InTable 1, panel A and Online Appendices B and C, we provide a list of UTSA enactment dates and empirical evidence on determinants of the UTSA. We do not find that enactment of the UTSA is significantly determined by state-level factors. Data We obtain our data from different sources. We derive site-level information on U.S. firms’ EMS from the CI database provided by Aberdeen (formerly Harte Hanks), a commercial market research firm. To collect these data, Aberdeen surveys IT managers about the type of hardware and software used at their sites. These data are sold to large IT firms (e.g., IMB, Cisco, or Dell), indicating that high timeliness and data quality as errors would be quickly detected during sales calls. The data have been widely used to study the adoption of IT (Forman 2005,Pincus et al. 2017) or implications of IT investment (Kleis et al. 2012,Bloom et al. 2014,Tian and Xu 2015,Pincus et al. 2017,Nagle 2019). For our empirical tests, we aggregate the site-level survey data to the firm level8and merge the firm-level EMS data with firm-level financial information from Compustat. We provide more evidence on the CI database and our sample in Online Appendix D. 3.4. Regression Model To test the effect of the UTSA onEMS integration, we estimate the following regression: Our fixed effects structure accounts for any time-invariant differences across firms via firm fixed effects (∂i) and for industry-level shocks via industry-year fixed effects (γj,t). Because our treatment variable,UTSA, is defined at the state level, we cluster standard errors by state. We include a set of firm-specific control variables,𝑿𝑖,𝑡Xi,t, that have been associated with EMS implementation in prior studies (Pincus et al. 2017). Specifically, we control for firm size proxied by the natural logarithm of one plus sales (log(1 +Sales)), the natural logarithm of one plus the number of employees (log(1 +Employees)), and the natural logarithm of one plus the number of sites (log(1 +#Sites)) as well as for IT intensity proxied by the number of personal computers (PCs) per employee (PCs/employee). Further, we include an indicator variable for the use of a Big 4 audit firm (Big 4), average sales growth (Avg. sales growth), environmental uncertainty (Uncertainty), and unrelated firm diversification (Unrelated diversification). We further include a set of time-varying, state-specific control variables,𝑿𝑠,𝑡Xs,t. These variables capture contemporaneous regulatory and macroeconomic developments at the state level that could affect firms’ information leakage risk. Specifically, we include indicator variables for whether a firm’s headquarters state adopted the inevitable disclosure doctrine (IDD) (based onKlasa et al. 2018),9enacted the data breach notification law (Data Breach) (based onKamiya et al. 2021), and a noncompete enforceability index (NCI) (adopted fromGarmaise 2011). Moreover, we include gross domestic product (GDP) growth and the level of unemployment to address the concern that an omitted economic characteristic could be correlated with both the UTSA andEMS integration. Finally, we also include the state-level share of employees working in the IT service sector to control for potential variation in the supply of EMSs. Equation (1)resembles a DID specification. Our coefficient of interest is𝛽β, capturing average changes inEMS integrationby firms located in UTSA enactment states relative to contemporaneous changes inEMS integrationby firms located in control states. If trade secret protection laws increase firms’ internal information integration, we expect𝛽βto be positive and statistically significant.
article.4. Results: 4.1. Descriptive Statistics Our sample starts in 1999 and ends in 2009 given the coverage of the CI database. We start our sample selection with 15,823 observations of nonfinancial U.S. firms from the CI database that we merge with Compustat based on firm name or ticker information. After dropping singletons and observations with missing values for the regression variables, we are left with 15,243 firm-year observations (corresponding to 1,916 unique firms). In our sample, 1,058 observations from two distinct states (namely, Pennsylvania and Tennessee) are exposed to an enactment of the UTSA during our sample period, corresponding to 7% of our sample (panel A ofTable 1).10 Panel B ofTable 1shows summary statistics of our main variables. Our dependent variable,EMS integration, is the share of sites that are integrated into a firm’s EMS, weighted by its number of employees. The average firm integrates about half of its sites into an EMS. In our empirical tests, we focus on the within-firm variation inEMS integration, which mainly reflects firms’ decision to integrate additional sites into an existing EMS rather than first-time installments of the EMS (see Online Appendix E for details). Given that larger sites are more likely to be integrated into an EMS, our employee-weighted construction ofEMS integrationis slightly higher (56%) than an equally weighted variable (i.e., the percentage share of integrated sites; 49%).11Further, the average firm in our sample has sales of about $3,567 million, about 15,591 employees, about 19 sites, and 0.66 PCs per employee. For a comparison between our sample and the Compustat universe, please refer to Online Appendix D. Panel C ofTable 1reports the Pearson correlation coefficients between variables.EMS integrationcorrelates with several firm-level variables. In particular, larger firms and firms with higher IT intensity exhibit higherEMS integration. Figure 1shows a generally increasing time trend in firms’EMS integration. At the beginning of our sample period, about 30% of sites are integrated into firms’ EMS on average. Until 2005,EMS integrationroughly doubles and then stagnates at a high level of 75%. At the same time, we observe substantial cross-sectional variation inEMS integrationas shown by overall wide yearly interquartile ranges, especially early in our sample period. Over time, interquartile ranges decrease as more firms integrate more sites into firms’ EMSs, raising the lower quartile ofEMS integration. Panel A ofTable 1reveals that none of the UTSA-treated states adopted the IDD or experienced a change in the NCI during our sample period. Accordingly, any correlation evident in panel C ofTable 1reflects that several states have adopted both the UTSA and the IDD before our sample period. Although the UTSA-treated states adopted the data breach notification law during our sample period, these events do not coincide closely (as there is a gap of two or five years, respectively). Furthermore, the enactment of data breach notification laws likely attenuates the relation between the UTSA and firms’ internal information integration as it increases the expected costs of information leakages. Overall, our descriptive statistics do not suggest that the adoption of the IDD, the adoption of the data breach notification law, or changes in the NCI are correlated with the enactment of the UTSA. 4.2. The Effect of the UTSA on EMS Integration InFigure 2, we present event-time plots illustrating the effect of trade secret protection laws on firms’ internal information integration followingBarrios (2022). The figure shows lead-lag coefficients from a two-way fixed effects regression (including firm and industry-year fixed effects but no linear controls) ofUTSAonEMS integration. Thexaxis presents the distance in years from the year in which a firm’s headquarters state adopted the UTSA. We use the year prior to the UTSA enactment as a baseline. The figure shows a strong increase (relative to the baseline year) in coefficients around UTSA enactments (i.e., from event-yeart= 0 onward). Further, the coefficients stay at elevated levels during the three years following the UTSA enactment. Thus, the figure provides prima facie evidence of a positive and persistent effect ofUTSAonEMS integration.12 We complement this evidence with the DID regression results reported inTable 2. Information Leakage Costs Channel In this section, we provide empirical evidence substantiating the channel through which we predict that trade secret protection laws affect firms’ internal information integration: the costs of information leakage. To this end, we differentiate between firms whose internal information integration is primarily determined by marginal costs versus marginal benefits. If trade secret protection laws affect firms’ internal information integration via leakage costs (as we predict), we expect firms for which marginal costs (benefits) matter more to increase their internal information integration more (less) in response to the UTSA. This follows as a reduction in leakage costs should have a stronger (weaker) impact for firms with higher marginal costs (benefits) of internal information integration. To explore the differential effects of the UTSA with respect to firms’ marginal costs/benefits of internal information integration, we estimate the following cross-sectional regressions: To capture differences in firms’marginal costsof internal information integration, we use two measures of external competition. Trade secrets are likely more important for firms facing stronger competition from existing rivals (Eisenhardt and Martin 2000,Li and Li 2020). Accordingly, we expect firms to incur greater marginal costs from internal information integration and thus, to respond more strongly to the UTSA when they face greater external competitive pressure. Our first measure of external competition is based on management’s discussion of competition in their 10-K filings (Competition) followingLi et al. (2013). Specifically, the measure is the ratio between the number of references to competition and the total number of words in a 10-K filing. Our second measure is firms’ technological peer pressure (TPP) followingCao et al. (2018), which more specifically captures firms’ exposure to technological competition. Moreover, we expect firms to exhibit higher marginal costs of internal information integration when they belong to a high-tech industry. These firms typically have more valuable inside information, making information leakage particularly costly (Aboody and Lev 2000). FollowingFrancis and Schipper (1999),High Techindicates whether a firm belongs to a high-tech industry. Firms with greater organizational complexity typically exhibit largermarginal benefitsof internal information integration. This follows as more complex firms face a greater need for internal coordination (Godigbe et al. 2022), and internal information integration can improve coordination within a firm (Labro et al. 2022). We proxy for organizational complexity using vertical integration as measured byFrésard et al. (2020). Vertical integration reflects the potential of a firm’s products to be vertically integrated to other products offered by the same firm. Firms with greater vertical integration will likely decide on their internal information integration primarily because of their (larger) marginal benefits. Accordingly, we expect them to respondlessto the shift in marginal costs because of the UTSA.16 Table 4reports our cross-sectional results. Columns (1) and (2) inTable 4show that the coefficients on the interaction termsUTSA××CompetitionandUTSA××TPPare positive and statistically significant at the 5% level and 10% level, respectively, indicating a stronger effect of the UTSA for firms facing more competition. Similarly, the coefficient on the interaction termUTSA××High-tech(column (3) inTable 4) is positive and statistically significant at the 10% level, indicating a stronger effect of the UTSA for firms belonging to the high-tech industry. The coefficient on the interaction termUTSA××Vertical Integration(column (4) inTable 4) is, on the other hand, negative and statistically significant at the 5% level, indicating that the effect of the UTSA is weaker for firms with greater vertical integration. Overall, these cross-sectional results are consistent with information leakage costs as the channel through which trade secret protection laws affect firms’ internal information integration. The Effect of the UTSA on Firms’ Internal Information Environment via EMS Integration In our main tests, we focus on the effect of the UTSA onEMS integration. Via its effect onEMS integration, the UTSA may also enrich the quality of firms’ internal information environment. This follows as internal information integration is an important input to firms’ IIQ and DMQ (e.g.,Bouwens and Abernethy 2000). However, the UTSA may also affect IIQ and DMQ via other channels thanEMS integration. For instance, it could reduce information spillovers from local peer firms (Hellmann and Perotti 2011,Li and Sandino 2018), which could offset a positive information effect of greaterEMS integrationand ultimately, even decrease IIQ and DMQ. Hence, the overall relation between UTSA and these outcomes of firms’ internal information environment is a priori unclear. In this section, we investigate the direct and indirect (viaEMS integration) paths fromUTSAto firms’ internal information environment captured by several proxies of firms’ IIQ and DMQ, respectively. For IIQ, we use four proxies: (1) collaboration culture (Chen et al. 2022), (2) earnings announcement speed (Brazel and Dang 2008,Gallemore and Labro 2015,Godigbe et al. 2022), (3) absence of restatements because of unintentional errors (Hennes et al. 2008,Gallemore and Labro 2015,Chen et al. 2018), and (4) accrual quality (Dechow and Dichev 2002,Morris and Venkatesh 2010,Labro et al. 2022). We expect firms with greaterEMS integrationto exhibit (1) a more collaborative corporate culture because they have the necessary technology to facilitate information exchange, enabling a strong communicative and collaborative environment. We further expect firms with higherEMS integrationto have (2) higher earnings announcement speed, (3) a lower likelihood of restatements because of unintentional errors, and (4) a higher accrual quality because of better integrated and streamlined reporting processes, enabling them to produce high-quality information more quickly (Chen et al. 2018,Labro et al. 2022). For DMQ, we use three proxies: (1) capex efficiency (Goodman et al. 2014), (2) labor efficiency (Pinnuck and Lillis 2007,Jung et al. 2014), and (3) total factor productivity (Imrohoroglu and Tuzel 2014). If firms with higherEMS integrationhave better information for decision making, we expect them to allocate their resources more efficiently, as reflected by higher values of these three variables. We perform path analyses and specify the following structural equation models of the UTSA’s direct and indirect relations (viaEMS integration) to IIQ and DMQ outcomes, respectively: Equation (3)resembles our main specification, where𝛽βreflects the direct path fromUTSAtoEMS integration.Equation (4)regresses bothUTSAandEMS integrationon the IIQ and DMQ outcomes defined, where𝛼α(𝛿δ) captures the direct path betweenUTSA(EMS integration) and these outcomes. Finally,𝛿δ×𝛽βcaptures the indirect path (viaEMS integration) fromUTSAto these outcomes. In both equations, we use the same controls and fixed effects as in our main test.17FollowingZhao et al. (2010), we bootstrap standard errors. If the UTSA affects IIQ and DMQ, respectively, viaEMS integration, we expect the products of𝛽β(the direct path fromUTSAtoEMS integration) and𝛿δ(the direct path fromEMS integrationto our outcome variables) to be positive and significant (𝛽×𝛿>0β×δ>0). Table 5shows the results of our path analyses. As in our main result, we document a significantly positive relation betweenUTSAandEMS integrationacross all specifications, consistent with a positive impact of trade secret protection laws on firms’ internal information integration. We, however, fail to document a significant direct path fromUTSAto almost all proxies of IIQ or DMQ, with the only exception of a positive relation betweenUTSAand earnings announcement speed (two-tailedp= 0.08). These findings suggest that the UTSA has no consistentdirecteffect on the internal information environment. At the same time, we consistently observe a positive direct path fromEMS integrationto almost all IIQ and DMQ outcomes (except for capex efficiency, which is marginally insignificant with a two-tailedp-value of 0.12). This finding is consistent with the notion thatEMS integrationis an important input factor to the quality of firms’ internal information environment. Similarly, we document a positive and significant indirect path (𝛿δ×𝛽)β), viaEMS integration, fromUTSAto almost all of our outcomes, except for earnings announcement speed (two-tailedp= 0.15) and capex efficiency (two-tailedp= 0.14). In conclusion, the results of our path analyses highlight that the overall effect of the UTSA on firms’ internal information environment is ambiguous, consistent with its nuanced effects on firms’ external information environment documented inGlaeser (2018). By contrast, we find evidence for a positive relation betweenEMS integrationand IIQ/DMQ as well as a positive indirect relation between the UTSA and IIQ/DMQ viaEMS integration. Robustness Tests: In this section, we probe the robustness of our results with regard to two key concerns: first, that the relation betweenUTSAandEMS integrationis confounded by omitted correlated variables and second, that the UTSA affects firms’ information integration via channels other than leakage costs. 5.1. Omitted Correlated Variables In our main analyses, we include a granular fixed effects structure, time-varying control variables, and event-time analyses documenting a common trend between treatment and control states prior to UTSA enactments. This approach restricts confounding factors to time-varying firm-level or state-level factors that change around states’ enactments of the UTSA and are not accounted for by our control variables. In this section, we further probe the robustness of our main finding to omitted variables and potential confounding events. 5.1.1. Treatment Effects by Individual State. In our main tests, we pool the effect of the UTSA onEMS integrationacross all firms headquartered in states that enact the law during our sample period. Because our treatment effect originates from only two states, there is a concern that contemporaneous events occurring in either one of these states may confound our results. To address this concern, we document that the effect of the UTSA holds for each treatment state individually (Table 6, panel A).18If our inferences were affected by a confounding event, such an event would need to take place in each of our treatment states at different points in time, coinciding with each state’s enactment of the UTSA (Wang and Burke 2022). Sensitivity to Choices of Control States. By the start of our sample period, most states have already enacted the UTSA. Thus, one might be concerned that there are fundamental differences between our treatment states (that enact the UTSA during our sample period) and those control states that had already enacted the UTSA by the beginning of our sample period. Similarly, our control states are exposed to other regulatory events during our sample period (Table 1, panel A), which could also affect our inferences. To address these concerns, we followHam and Ueda (2021)and re-estimateEquation (1)using different sets of control states. First, we restrict our set of control states to those that had not enacted the UTSA by 1999 (the beginning of our sample period).19Second, we drop states from the control group that experience other regulatory events during our sample period. Specifically, we exclude firms from control states that during our sample period, adopt the IDD, exhibit a change in their noncompete enforcement index, or enact a data breach notification law. Our findings in columns (2)–(4) in panel B ofTable 6remain unchanged. Similarly, we find that the parallel trend assumption holds across all four sets of control states (untabulated). Collectively, these findings refute the notion that our inferences are affected by differences between treatment and control firms. 5.1.3. Data Breach Notification Laws. Next, we corroborate our main finding with an alternative event that we expect, like the enactment of the UTSA, to affect firms’ information leakage costs: the enactment of data breach disclosure notification laws. These laws require firms to notify affected parties (e.g., consumers) of a data breach after its discovery (Romanosky et al. 2011,Chen et al. 2019), thereby imposing costs on firms in the form of reputation concerns (Romanosky et al. 2011), increased stakeholder concerns about data cyber risk management (Chen et al. 2019), and investments in information security (Romanosky et al. 2011). That is, data breach notification laws increase the costs of information leakages resulting from data breaches, which in turn, may incentivize firms to reduceEMS integration. Thus, compared with the UTSA, we expect data breach notification laws to affectEMS integrationin the opposite direction. In column (3) ofTable 2, the coefficient onData breachcaptures the effect of data breach notification laws onEMS integration(seeKamiya et al. 2021andTable 1, panel A for an overview of the (relevant) effective enactment dates). We find a significant negative relation betweenData breachandEMS integration. This finding mitigates concerns that our main result is driven by unobservable factors correlated with states’ enactment of the UTSA because such factors would also need to correlate in the opposite direction with the enactment of data breach notification laws. It further refutes the notion thatEMS integrationreflects firms’ decision to enhance information security rather than information integration, in line with the evidence inBloom et al. (2014).20 5.1.4. State-Level UTSA Determinants. FollowingKim et al. (2021), we further explore the concern that state-level correlated omitted variables are driving our results. We do this by regressingUTSAonAvg. EMS integration, which is the average ofEMS integrationacross all firms in a given state over the past three years, and several other state-level characteristics for the same three-year average. The results presented in Online Appendix C indicate that neitherAvg. EMS integrationnor other state-level characteristics have any predictive power for the UTSA. In line with this,Ribstein and Kobayashi (1996)present evidence that the adoption of the UTSA is not driven by lobbying interests but rather, is associated with efficiency benefits of uniformity and the effort of the Commissioners on Uniform State Laws (Glaeser 2018). Overall, these results lend support to the notion that UTSA enactment is plausibly exogenous and does not stem from pre-exiting state-level characteristics or EMS integration. 5.1.5. Site-Level Factors. We perform our tests at the firm level because first, the applicability of the UTSA is strongly determined by a firm’s headquarters state (seeSection 3.2) and second, decisions to integrate sites into an EMS are typically made by the firm’s top management rather than individual sites (Cheng et al. 2021). Yet, one might be concerned that the integration of a site into an EMS is also importantly determined by site-level characteristics, such as its size or IT sophistication, which are not appropriately captured by our firm-level average control variables and add noise to our empirical tests. To assess the sensitivity of our results with respect to site-level characteristics, we adopt the approach ofPierri and Timmer (2020)and measure firms’EMS integrationcontrolling for site-level aspects that are potential confounders of firms’ decision to integrate internal information. Specifically, we construct an alternative measure ofEMS integrationbased on year-by-year site-level regressions of a site’s integration into an EMS on a firm fixed effect while controlling for several site-level characteristics. Specifically, our controls include the size of the site (proxied by the natural logarithm of one plus the number of employees at the site level), its IT sophistication (proxied by the number of personal computers divided by the number employees at the site level), and location (i.e., site-state fixed effects accounting for, for example, differences in local taxes as well as labor and product markets). Our alternative measure,EMS integrationFE, is provided by the firm fixed effects from these regressions. Notably, this measure is purged of the variation arising from the aforementioned site-level characteristics (see alsoBeaudry et al. 2010for a similar approach measuring IT adoption at a regional level).Table 6, panel C displays our results. As expected, we find that larger sites and sites with a higher degree of IT sophistication are significantly more likely to be integrated into a firm’s EMS (column (1) in panel C ofTable 6). Notably, accounting for these characteristics does not change our inferences with respect to the effect of the UTSA onEMS integration(column (2) in panel C ofTable 6). This finding does not support a major confounding effect of site-level characteristics on our main finding. 5.1.6. Impact of Potential Omitted Correlated Variables. Finally, we quantify the impact of potential omitted correlated variables followingFrank (2000),Larcker and Rusticus (2010), andBusenbark et al. (2022). Specifically, we compute the ITCV to determine the minimum impact required for an omitted correlated variable to invalidate our inferences. The impact of a variable is defined as the product of its partial correlations (after the other control variables and fixed effects) withUTSAandEMS integration, respectively. In untabulated tests, we find that the ITCV forUTSAis 0.0116 at the 10% significance level. This means that the impact of an omitted correlated variable would need to exceed 0.0116 to invalidate our inferences. To put this threshold into perspective, we examine the impact scores of the control variables from our main model. Among these variables,IDDexhibits the highest impact score with 0.0021. Notably, the threshold value exceeds this score by more than 5.5 times. Similarly, the RIR analysis suggests that 46% of the parameter estimate ofUTSAwould have to be because of bias to invalidate our inferences at the 10% level. Assuming that we include a reasonable set of control variables in our model, both tests suggest that it is unlikely that an omitted correlated variable exists that would render our main results insignificant if included in our main model (Busenbark et al. 2022,Drake et al. 2022). 5.2. Alternative Channels Another concern is that the UTSA might affect firms’ internal information integration via channels other than leakage cost. To address this concern, we first present analyses corroborating the leakage costs channel by using an alternative dependent variable, which captures firms’ internal information integration after accounting for other investments into information system. Second, we probe the sensitivity of our results to two specific alternative channels, namely firms’ external reporting incentives and financial constraints. 5.2.1. Other Investments in Information System. First, we address the concern that the UTSA might incentivize managers to invest in firms’ overall information systems (not only in those integrated into an EMS) for reasons other than reduced leakage costs (such as alleviated financial constraints or increased incentives to digitalize information). To that end, we leverage our granular data on specific software applications. Specifically, we examine the effect of the UTSA on firms’ use of three specific software applications: ACC, HR, and CRM.21These applications can be part of an EMS or installed as a stand-alone application. A key difference between integrated (EMS) and stand-alone applications is that EMSs enable informationintegrationacross sites and functions, whereas stand-alone applications only allow for the exchange of information across sites within a given function (e.g.,Ranganathan and Brown 2006,Peng and Gala 2014). For each application, we measure an integration ratio defined as the share of sites (weighted by employees) that integrate a specific application (i.e., installed as part of an EMS) relative to the total number of sites that use this specific application (either as a stand-alone application or integrated into the EMS). This is akin to defining an application-specific EMS integration variable and scaling it by the general importance of the application for a given firm. Doing so allows us to focus on the specific effect of the UTSA on firms’ decisions to integrate information into their EMS, relative to general, firm-specific trends in software applications. If stronger trade secret protection incentivizes firms to generally invest in information systems, we expect the implementation of all applications to increase, regardless of whether they are stand-alone or integrated into the EMS. By contrast, if stronger trade secret protection reduces leakage costs and thus, specifically incentivizes informationintegration, we expect that the use of integrated software applications increases disproportionally to nonintegrated, stand-alone software applications. We re-estimateEquation (1)for each of our three software integration ratios (ACC, HR, and CRM). Consistent with our hypothesis and main result,UTSAis positively related to the integration ratios across all three applications (Table 7). This also implies that any potential alternative mechanisms would need to specifically affect the degree ofinformation integrationrelative to firm-specific trends in the use of software applications. FE, fixed effect. **Two-tailed significance levels at the 5% level; ***two-tailed significance levels at the 1% level. 5.2.2. Financial Reporting Incentives. One specific concern might be that the UTSA impacts firms’ external reporting incentives, stimulating them to increase internal information integration. This channel is, however, not unequivocal but rather, multifaceted. On the one hand, stronger trade secret protection might reduce firms’ external reporting incentives as the incremental proprietary costs of public disclosure are higher (Glaeser 2018,Li et al. 2018). On the other hand, whereas stronger trade secret protection incentivizes firms to be more publicly opaque with respect to their trade secrets, it could stimulate their external reporting of nonproprietary information that is unrelated to their trade secrets. This follows as firms might aim to counteract the increase in information asymmetry because of their trade secret-related opaqueness (Glaeser 2018). To assess the importance of this channel, we estimate the controlled direct effect followingAcharya et al. (2016). This approach provides us with an estimate of the effect of the UTSA onEMS integrationnet of the effect of an alternative channel. If the UTSA affectsEMS integrationonly via external reporting incentives, the controlled direct effect will be insignificant. To proxy for the external reporting incentives, we use the richness of firms’ external information environment measured by analysts followingFiechter et al. (2022). We find that the controlled direct effect of the UTSA onEMS integrationis significant and almost identical in size to our baseline effect (untabulated). This effect reflects the relation betweenUTSAandEMS integrationafter purging the estimate off the effects of the analyst following onEMS integration. Whereas this cannot rule out that the UTSA also operates via channels other than leakage costs, it does not support the notion that external reporting incentives are an important mechanism through which the UTSA affects firms’ internal information integration. 5.2.3. Financial Constraints. Second, trade secret protection laws may increase (incumbent) firms’ profitability, alleviating financial constraints and enabling larger investments, including investments in EMSs. We note that our tests on application integration ratios (Table 7) in the previous section already mitigate this concern; if the UTSA generally enables firms to spend more resources on their IT, we expect a similar increase in integrated and stand-alone applications. Yet, we find that firms specifically increase the share of applications that are integrated in an EMS. To further assess the importance of this channel, we also estimate the controlled direct effect followingAcharya et al. (2016). FollowingKlasa et al. (2018), we measure financial constraints by return on assets. Again, we find that the controlled direct effect of the UTSA onEMS integrationis significant and very similar in size to our baseline effect (untabulated). This effect reflects the relation betweenUTSAandEMS integrationafter purging the estimate of the effects return on assets onEMS integration. Together, our tests do not support the notion that financial constraints are an important mechanism via which the UTSA affects firms’ internal information integration.
article.6. Conclusion: Using proprietary data, we introduce a novel proxy for firms’ internal information integration: the share of sites weighted by the number of employees that are integrated into a firm’s EMS (EMS integration). We exploit the staggered enactment of the UTSA to capture shifts in the marginal costs of internal information integration. Our findings suggest thatEMS integrationincreases as the expected costs of information leakage decrease because of stronger trade secret protection under the UTSA. We further provide evidence that the UTSA-induced increase in internal information integration translates into improvements in firms’ internal information quality and decision-making quality. Our findings contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of firms’ internal information environment. Specifically, we shed light on how an important strategic cost factor matters for a specific input to firms’ internal information environment. There are some potential limitations relating to our data and sample period. First, the technologies used during our sample period differ from the technologies available today (e.g., machine learning, big data, cloud computing), potentially reducing the generalizability of our findings. At the same time, our sample period covers a time when EMS adoption was growing rapidly but concerns for data leakages were similarly high (Peng and Gala 2014), providing a suitable setting to examine the cost-benefit trade-off motivating our study. Second, our data do not allow us to examine potentially different levels of quality of EMS and their actual usage by managers. Nonetheless, our path analyses indicate that, on average, internal information integration via EMS improves internal information and decision-making quality. Lastly, firms might try to protect trade secrets via other mechanisms (such as organizational design). It would be interesting for future research to investigate how these choices interact with the integration of internal information.
